title: Wills and Estate Settlement
description: Understand the elements of a valid will, types of wills, probate process and alternatives, and intestacy implications.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Wills are the foundational estate planning document, specifying how a person's assets should be distributed after death. Understanding valid will requirements, the probate process, and what happens without a will is essential for CFP professionals.

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Most clients have wills, but many are outdated or improperly executed. Knowing valid will requirements helps you identify potential estate planning gaps.

sections.title: Elements of a Valid Will
rows: Sound mind; understand nature of assets and heirs
Document intended to be final disposition

sections.title: Types of Wills
sections.content:
Formal (Attested) Will: Typed, signed, witnessed—most legally sound.

Holographic Will: Entirely in testator's handwriting; no witnesses required in some states; higher risk of challenge.

Nuncupative (Oral) Will: Spoken, usually on deathbed; very limited acceptance; usually only for personal property.

Pour-Over Will: Directs assets into existing trust; catches assets not in trust at death.

sections.title: Key Will Provisions
items: Specific Bequests: "I give my 1965 Mustang to my son, Michael."
General Bequests: "I give $50,000 to my daughter, Sarah."
Residuary Clause: "All remaining assets go to my spouse, Jane."
Simultaneous Death Clause: Addresses what happens if spouses die together
Tax Apportionment Clause: Specifies how estate taxes are allocated

sections.title: Probate Process Steps
sections.content:
1. File will with probate court
2. Appoint personal representative/executor
3. Inventory and appraise assets
4. Notify creditors (advertisement period)
5. Pay debts and taxes
6. Distribute remaining assets
7. Close estate

sections.title: Avoiding Probate
items: Revocable living trusts
Joint tenancy with right of survivorship
Beneficiary designations (IRAs, life insurance)
TOD/POD accounts
Gifts during lifetime

sections.title: Intestacy
sections.content: When no valid will exists, state laws determine distribution (varies by state). Typical intestate distribution: Surviving spouse receives portion (1/2 to all); children share remaining equally; if no spouse/children: parents, siblings, etc. Ultimate fallback: Escheat to state.

sections.title: Exam Warning
sections.content: Beneficiary designations supersede will provisions. If a client's IRA names an ex-spouse, the ex-spouse receives the IRA regardless of what the will says. This is a common exam trap and real-world planning mistake.

sections.title: Mnemonic: WRITE
sections.content: Will Requirements: Written, Rational (capacity), Intent, Two witnesses, Executed (signed)

sections.title: Summary
items: Valid will requires capacity, intent, writing, signature, and witnesses
Pour-over wills work with revocable trusts to catch missed assets
Probate is public, time-consuming, and expensive
Joint tenancy and beneficiary designations avoid probate
Intestacy laws may not reflect your actual wishes

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is NOT a requirement for a valid formal (attested) will in most jurisdictions?
options: The testator must be of sound mind.
The will must be in writing.
The will must be signed by the testator in the presence of witnesses.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: While notarization provides additional verification, it is not universally required for a formal (attested) will to be considered valid. The core requirements typically include being in writing, signed by a testator of sound mind, and witnessed.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the core elements that ensure a will's authenticity and enforceability.

rows.label: Form
values: Typically typed or printed
Entirely handwritten by the testator

rows.label: Witnesses
values: Requires witnesses
Generally does not require witnesses (depending on state law)

rows.label: Legal Soundness
values: Generally considered more legally sound due to documentation and witness attestation
More susceptible to challenges due to potential ambiguity or questions about authenticity

reveal.question: Describe the typical limitations associated with a nuncupative (oral) will.
reveal.answer: Nuncupative wills are generally subject to strict limitations. They are often only valid for the transfer of personal property (not real estate), are only recognized in specific circumstances (e.g., during a testator's last illness or imminent peril), and often require prompt reduction to writing by witnesses to be considered valid.

title: Powers of Attorney and Healthcare Directives
description: Distinguish between types of powers of attorney, understand durable vs. non-durable powers, and explain healthcare proxy documents.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Powers of attorney are legal documents authorizing someone (agent/attorney-in-fact) to act on your behalf for financial or legal matters. These documents are critical for incapacity planning.

sections.title: Why Durable POA is Essential
sections.content: Without a durable power of attorney, court guardianship may be needed. Guardianship is expensive, public, and restrictive. A durable POA avoids court involvement entirely.

sections.title: Types of Powers of Attorney
sections.content:
General Power of Attorney: Broad authority over financial matters; ends at incapacity or death.

Limited/Special Power of Attorney: Specific purpose or time period (e.g., real estate closing).

Durable Power of Attorney: Continues during incapacity; essential for planning; must specifically state durability.

Springing Power of Attorney: "Springs" into effect upon incapacity; requires medical certification; delays can be problematic.

sections.title: Choosing an Agent
items: Trustworthy and competent
Geographically accessible
Willing to serve
Consider successor agents

sections.title: Healthcare Directives Overview
sections.content:
Healthcare Proxy (Healthcare POA): Appoints agent for medical decisions; activates when you cannot communicate; agent follows your known wishes.

Living Will: States treatment preferences; focus on end-of-life situations; specifically addresses life-sustaining treatment. Living will triggers: terminal condition, permanent unconsciousness, or end-stage condition.

HIPAA Authorization: Allows access to medical information; without it, providers may not share info.

DNR Orders: Medical order (not advance directive); signed by physician; prevents CPR if heart stops.

sections.title: Complete Incapacity Plan
items: Durable Power of Attorney (financial)
Healthcare Proxy (medical decisions)
Living Will (treatment preferences)
HIPAA Authorization (information access)
Revocable Trust (asset management)

sections.title: Exam Warning
sections.content: 70% of people over 65 will need long-term care. Cognitive decline affects decision-making, and court intervention is expensive and public. All clients need incapacity planning documents.

sections.title: Mnemonic: PAHL
sections.content: Incapacity Documents: POA (financial), Advance directive, Healthcare proxy, Living will

sections.title: Summary
items: Durable POA is essential—it survives incapacity
Without POA, court guardianship may be required
Healthcare proxy appoints a medical decision-maker
Living will states end-of-life treatment preferences
Complete incapacity planning needs all four documents

sections.title: Durable Power of Attorney Knowledge Check

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements is CORRECT regarding a Durable Power of Attorney?
options: It automatically terminates upon the principal's incapacity.
It grants broad authority over all of the principal's affairs, including healthcare decisions.
It must explicitly state that it survives the principal's incapacity to remain valid during that period.
It is only valid for a specified period, regardless of the principal's capacity.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A Durable Power of Attorney must specifically state that it remains in effect even if the principal becomes incapacitated. This is the key feature that distinguishes it from a non-durable power of attorney. Healthcare decisions are typically covered under a separate healthcare proxy or advance directive.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what makes a Durable Power of Attorney 'durable'.

sections.title: Test Yourself: General Power of Attorney

reveal.question: When does a General Power of Attorney typically terminate?
reveal.answer: A General Power of Attorney terminates upon the principal's incapacity or death. It does NOT continue if the principal becomes incapacitated.

sections.title: Durable vs. Non-Durable Power of Attorney

rows.label: Effectiveness During Incapacity
values: Remains effective if the principal becomes incapacitated (if explicitly stated).
Terminates immediately upon the principal's incapacity.

rows.label: Requirement for Incapacity Clause
values: Must explicitly state that it survives incapacity.
No clause needed, as it automatically terminates upon incapacity.

rows.label: Use Case
values: Incapacity planning; managing affairs if the principal becomes unable to do so.
Managing affairs while the principal is available and competent, but unable to act (e.g., traveling). Often used when the principal is overseas.

title: Introduction to Trusts
description: Define trust terminology and parties, distinguish revocable from irrevocable trusts, and explain basic trust taxation.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: A trust is a legal arrangement where one party (trustee) holds property for the benefit of another (beneficiary). Trusts are fundamental estate planning tools used for probate avoidance, tax planning, and asset protection.

sections.title: Trust Parties
rows: Takes over if trustee cannot serve

sections.title: Trust Property Examples
items: Real estate
Bank accounts
Investment accounts
Life insurance (ILIT)
Business interests
Tangible personal property

sections.title: Revocable Living Trust
sections.content:
Characteristics: Grantor can modify or revoke; grantor typically serves as trustee; assets included in taxable estate; no income tax entity (grantor trust); no asset protection.

Benefits: Probate avoidance; privacy (not public record); incapacity management; flexibility to change.

Funding Requirements: Must transfer assets to trust; unfunded trust provides no benefit; pour-over will catches missed assets.

sections.title: Irrevocable Trust
sections.content: Characteristics: Cannot be modified/revoked by grantor; grantor gives up control; may be excluded from taxable estate; separate tax entity (may pay taxes); asset/creditor protection possible.

sections.title: Key Trade-off
sections.content: "Control vs. Tax Benefits"—Less control = More tax advantages. This is the fundamental trade-off in choosing between revocable and irrevocable trusts.

sections.title: Exam Warning
sections.content: Trust tax brackets are severely compressed! Trusts reach the top 37% bracket at only $15,950 vs. $626,350 for individuals. This is a critical exam concept.

sections.title: Trust Taxation Types
sections.content:
Grantor Trust: Income taxed to grantor; trust is "ignored" for tax purposes; no separate tax return required (optional). Example: Revocable living trust.

Simple Trust: Must distribute all income annually; cannot make charitable contributions; cannot distribute principal; beneficiaries pay tax on distributions.

Complex Trust: Can accumulate income; can make charitable contributions; can distribute principal; taxed on retained income.

Distribution Deduction: Trust gets deduction for income distributed to beneficiaries. Beneficiaries include distribution in their income.

sections.title: Common Trust Purposes
items: Probate Avoidance: Assets in trust are not probated; immediate access for beneficiaries; privacy maintained
Incapacity Management: Successor trustee takes over; no court involvement; seamless asset management
Beneficiary Protection: Spendthrift provisions; creditor protection; professional management
Tax Planning: Estate tax reduction (irrevocable); GST tax planning; charitable giving
Special Needs: Preserve government benefits; supplemental needs trust; professional management

sections.title: Mnemonic: GTB
sections.content: Trust Parties: Grantor (creates), Trustee (manages), Beneficiary (receives)

sections.title: Summary
items: Trust parties: Grantor creates, Trustee manages, Beneficiary receives
Revocable trusts avoid probate but offer no tax benefits
Irrevocable trusts may reduce estate taxes but require giving up control
Trust tax brackets are severely compressed—top rate at $15,950
Funding the trust is essential—unfunded trusts provide no benefit

sections.title: Revocable vs. Irrevocable Trusts: Test Your Knowledge

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is a primary disadvantage of using a revocable living trust compared to an irrevocable trust for estate planning purposes?
options: Assets held in a revocable living trust are generally not protected from the grantor's creditors.
A revocable living trust requires more complex tax reporting than an irrevocable trust.
The grantor of a revocable living trust loses all control over the trust assets.
Revocable living trusts are generally more expensive to establish than irrevocable trusts.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is A. Revocable trusts do not offer asset protection from the grantor's creditors because the grantor retains control and access to the assets. Irrevocable trusts, on the other hand, can provide asset protection if properly structured. B is incorrect because revocable trusts are grantor trusts, meaning the grantor's social security number is used and there is no separate tax reporting. C is incorrect because the grantor maintains control. D is incorrect because revocable trusts are generally cheaper to establish.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the level of control the grantor has over the trust assets in each type of trust.

reveal.question: What is the primary tax characteristic of a 'grantor trust' and why are revocable living trusts typically classified as such?
reveal.answer: In a grantor trust, the grantor (the person who created the trust) is treated as the owner of the trust assets for income tax purposes. This means that any income generated by the trust assets is taxed directly to the grantor's individual income tax return, as if the trust did not exist. Revocable living trusts are typically classified as grantor trusts because the grantor retains the power to control and revoke the trust, making them the 'owner' for tax purposes.

sections.title: Revocable vs. Irrevocable Trusts: A Quick Comparison

rows.label: Grantor Control
values: Grantor retains control; can modify or revoke
Grantor relinquishes control; cannot modify or revoke (generally)

rows.label: Estate Tax Inclusion
values: Assets included in grantor's taxable estate
Assets may be excluded from grantor's taxable estate (depending on structure)

rows.label: Asset Protection
values: Generally does not provide asset protection from grantor's creditors
May provide asset protection from grantor's creditors (depending on structure and state law)

rows.label: Taxation
values: Grantor trust; income taxed to grantor
May be a separate tax entity; may pay its own taxes

title: Types of Trusts
description: Identify major trust types and their purposes, understand when each trust type is appropriate, and compare trust structures.
topics: Trust structures for different planning goals

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Different trust types serve different planning purposes. Understanding when to use each type is essential for effective estate planning. This lesson covers estate tax reduction trusts, charitable trusts, and special purpose trusts.

sections.title: Credit Shelter Trust (Bypass/Family Trust)
sections.content:
Purpose: Maximize use of estate tax exemption at first spouse's death.

How It Works:
1. At first death, exemption amount ($7.0M in 2026, due to TCJA sunset) funds bypass trust
2. Surviving spouse can receive income and limited principal
3. At second death, bypass trust assets pass to children, NOT included in surviving spouse's estate

Key Features: Preserves deceased spouse's exemption; surviving spouse access without estate inclusion; growth occurs outside taxable estate.

Note: Portability has reduced need, but bypass trusts still valuable for state estate tax planning, creditor protection, and ensuring children receive assets.

sections.title: QTIP Trust (Qualified Terminable Interest Property)
sections.content:
Purpose: Provide for surviving spouse while controlling ultimate distribution.

Requirements:
• All income distributed to spouse at least annually
• No other beneficiaries during spouse's lifetime
• Executor must make QTIP election

Benefits: Control where assets go after spouse dies; marital deduction for estate tax; common in second marriage situations.

Example: Husband with children from first marriage leaves assets in QTIP for second wife. At her death, assets go to his children.

sections.title: Irrevocable Life Insurance Trust (ILIT)
sections.content:
Purpose: Remove life insurance from taxable estate.

How It Works:
1. Grantor creates irrevocable trust
2. Trust applies for and owns life insurance
3. Trust pays premiums (grantor makes gifts to trust)
4. At death, proceeds paid to trust, distributed per terms

Key Requirements: Grantor cannot be trustee; 3-year look-back on transferred policies; Crummey notices for gift tax exclusion.

sections.title: ILIT Estate Tax Savings
sections.content: Tax Savings = Policy Value × Estate Tax Rate. For a $2M policy at 40% estate tax rate = $800,000 savings!

sections.title: Charitable Remainder Trust (CRT)
sections.content:
Structure: Income to donor (or others) for life/term; remainder to charity.

Types:
• CRAT: Charitable Remainder Annuity Trust (fixed payments)
• CRUT: Charitable Remainder Unitrust (percentage of annual value)

Benefits: Income stream; charitable deduction; capital gains bypass; estate tax reduction.

sections.title: Charitable Lead Trust (CLT)
sections.content:
Structure: Income to charity for term; remainder to family.

Benefits: Reduce gift/estate tax on family transfer; support charity during term; "freeze" asset values.

sections.title: CRT vs CLT Memory Aid
sections.content:
CRT = Charity gets Remainder (you get income first)
CLT = Charity Leads (charity gets income first)

sections.title: Special Needs Trust (SNT)
sections.content:
Purpose: Supplement government benefits without disqualification.

Structure: Trustee has discretion over distributions; cannot provide food/shelter (would reduce SSI); supplements, not supplants, government benefits.

Types:
• First-party SNT: Funded with beneficiary's assets (Medicaid payback required)
• Third-party SNT: Funded by others (no payback)

sections.title: Spendthrift Trust
sections.content:
Purpose: Protect beneficiary from creditors and poor judgment.

Features: Beneficiary cannot assign/transfer interest; creditors cannot reach assets before distribution; trustee controls timing/amount of distributions.

sections.title: Dynasty Trust (Generation-Skipping Trust)
sections.content:
Purpose: Preserve wealth for multiple generations.

Features: Designed to last perpetually (where allowed); avoids estate tax at each generation; uses GST exemption.

State Laws: Must be created in state allowing perpetual trusts (e.g., Delaware, Nevada, South Dakota).

sections.title: Summary
items: Credit shelter trusts protect deceased spouse's exemption from estate tax
QTIP trusts balance marital deduction with control over ultimate distribution
ILITs remove life insurance from the taxable estate
CRTs provide income stream plus charitable deduction
Special needs trusts preserve government benefit eligibility

knowledgeCheck.question: A client, whose estate is projected to exceed the estate tax exemption amount, wants to ensure their surviving spouse is provided for while also maximizing the use of their applicable exclusion amount. Which type of trust would be MOST suitable to achieve this goal?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A Credit Shelter Trust, also known as a Bypass Trust, is designed to utilize the deceased spouse's estate tax exemption amount. Assets up to the exemption amount are placed in the trust, providing income and limited principal to the surviving spouse, while the assets avoid estate tax in both spouses' estates. While a QTIP trust provides for the surviving spouse, the assets are included in the surviving spouse's estate.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which trust directly leverages the estate tax exemption at the first spouse's death.

reveal.question: List the two primary requirements for a trust to qualify as a QTIP Trust.
reveal.answer: 1. All income must be distributed to the surviving spouse at least annually. 2. No other beneficiaries are permitted to receive distributions during the surviving spouse's lifetime.

sections.title: Credit Shelter vs. QTIP Trust: Key Differences

rows.label: Primary Goal
values: Maximize estate tax exemption use at first death; avoid estate tax on appreciation in the trust
Provide for surviving spouse while controlling ultimate distribution of assets; qualify for the marital deduction

rows.label: Inclusion in Surviving Spouse's Estate
values: Generally NOT included in surviving spouse's estate
Included in surviving spouse's estate

rows.label: Control of Ultimate Distribution
values: Assets pass to beneficiaries determined by the deceased spouse, bypassing the surviving spouse's estate
Assets pass to beneficiaries determined by the deceased spouse, but only after the surviving spouse's death; surviving spouse cannot alter the distribution

rows.label: Flexibility for Surviving Spouse
values: Less flexible; surviving spouse typically receives income and limited principal.
More flexible in terms of income distribution, but less flexible regarding who ultimately receives the assets.
